content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 29, 1989


Honorable Royall Brown
Director, Upper San Gabriel Valley
Municipal Water District
2153 Aroma Drive
West Covina, CA 91791

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-89-511

Dear Mr. Brown:

We have received your letter requesting advice concerning the
duties of Robert Nicholson under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹ Mr. Nicholson is a
director of the Upper San Gabriel Valley Municipal Water District.

The Commission only provides advice to the person whose
duties are in question or to his or her authorized representative.
(Section 83114(b); Regulation 18329, copy enclosed.) Your letter
does not indicate that Mr. Nicholson has authorized your request
for advice concerning his conduct. Accordingly, we cannot respond
to your questions. However, I have enclosed a pamphlet which
summarizes the conflict of interest laws. Also enclosed are two
advice letters which address questions that may be similar to the
ones you have asked. (Kennedy Advice Letter, No. A-85-194; Atigh
Advice Letter, No. I-86-270.)

If you believe that a violation of the conflict of interest
laws has occurred, you might consider filing a complaint with our
Enforcement Division. Enclosed is a copy of a complaint form.


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

If you have any questions concerning this letter, please
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

KED:plh

Enclosures

August 24, 1989

California Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Ref: Government Code 87100 (noncontractual conflicts of interest)

Dear Sir:

As a newly elected Director of the Upper San Gabriel Valley Municipal Water District, I would appreciate a written opinion concerning a controversy that was highlighted in the enclosed "Public Affairs Clipsheet" of August 4, 1989, Pasadena Star News

There are two questions which I would like answered by your agency.

1. A member of our Board of Directors also holds an ownership interest in the San Gabriel Valley Water Company. This water company sells water that it procure to retail customers within our district. The district is scheduled to vote on September 6, 1989 to create by resolution a "special fund" generated from the surplus money created due to the difference between the cost of water that we buy and the selling price. Should this member be allowed to vote on a policy decision regarding creation of this special fund?

2. Our district sells water to a Watermaster committee which then sells this water to the company of which the director is an officer. Can this same director vote to set water rates charged by the district?

The District Board of Directors asked out attorney, Author C. Kidman, for a written opinion to questions which might be very similar to the above. He should be contacting your office. However, I would appreciate it if you would send your answers to my questions directly to me.

Very truly yours,

Royall Brown
Royall Brown
2153 Aroma Drive
West Covina, CA 91791

PUBLIC AFFAIRS CLIPSHEET                    SPECIAL    PUBLICATION    Pasadena Star-News
Metropolitan Water District
of Southern California                              DATE    August 4, 1989

Water firm owner
denounced for
public agency role

BY JOHN FLECK
Staff Writer

EL MONTE — An El Monte
water company owner should
not be allowed to serve on the
board of the public agency that
sells his company its water, a
community activist said
Wednesday at a meeting of the
agency's board.

Wil Baca, a Hacienda Heights
engineer, said San Gabriel Val-
ley Water Company President
Robert H. Nicholson should not
be allowed to serve on the board
of the Upper San Gabriel Valley
Municipal Water District, the
agency that sells the imported
Northern California and Colora-
do River water used by Nichol-
son's company.

Nicholson was elected to the
board of the Water District,
which serves an area reaching
from Arcadia east to Azusa and
south to Whittier Narrows,
more than two years ago. But
Baca charged that Nicholson's
position as president of a com-
pany that buys water from the
district should prevent him
from serving on the board.

"They can't be on both sides of
the transaction," Baca said.
"They can't be both water sell-
ers and water buyers."

Baca's comments come at a
time when the Water District is
considering a change in water
prices that could give Nichol-
son's company an estimated
$700,000 per year windfall.

Nicholson could not be
reached Thursday for comment,
but agency attorney Mike
McCormick said Nicholson's
presence on the board does not
create a conflict of interest
under state law.

Nicholson's votes must have
"some direct and substantial
financial effect" on his company
for a conflict to exist, McCor-
mick said.

The Upper San Gabriel Valley
Municipal Water District serves
as one of two intermediaries
between the Metropolitan Water District of Southern Califor-
nia and local water companies.

The agency buys water from
the MWD and sells it to the
Main San Gabriel Basin Water-
master's office, which in turn

VALLEY WATERCOURSE

The bureaucratic path of
water on its way into the
South San Gabriel Valley:

■ The Metropolitan
Water District of Southern
California imports water
from Northern California
and the Colorado River,
selling it to

■ the Upper San Gabriel
Valley Municipal Water
District, a "paper agency"
that actually owns no pipes
or reservoirs, selling its
water (on paper) to

■ the Main San Gabriel
Basin Watermaster (run by
the same man who runs
the Water District), which
pours water from MWD
pipes into giant spreading
grounds, to be purchased
by

■ Robert Nicholson's
San Gabriel Valley Water
Company, which pumps
the water out of the ground
and sells it to consumers
throughout the valley.

sells the water to companies
such as Nicholson's.

Nicholson's financial disclo-
sure statements show that he
has an ownership interest in
United Resources Inc., which in
turn holds an ownership interest
in the San Gabriel Valley Water
Company.

In the past, the Water District
and the Watermaster have
charged whatever price the
MWD charges, neither raising
nor lowering it, said Bob Ber-
lien, who serves as head of staff
for both the Water District and
the Watermaster.

Baca raised the conflict of
interest issue after the MWD set
up a new program offering a
25 percent discount on water in
the winter, when the giant agen-
cy has surpluses. The Water
District is trying to decide
whether to keep the money
saved and set it aside for pollu-
tion cleanup projects or to pass
the savings on to companies
such as Nicholson's. A decision
is not expected until September

sections.question_synthetic: What are the conflict of interest duties applicable to Robert Nicholson, a director of the Upper San Gabriel Valley Municipal Water District, under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide advice because the request was made by a third party (the Director) rather than by Mr. Nicholson himself or his authorized representative, as required by Section 83114(b) and Regulation 18329.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination/refusal letter with no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections. The letter clearly states the basis for declining (lack of authorization from the interested party). Handwritten notes at bottom appear to describe water district structure and potential conflict but are not part of the formal FPPC response. Document structured as a business letter format rather than formal advice letter format.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the conflict of interest duties applicable to Robert Nicholson, a director of the Upper San Gabriel Valley Municipal Water District, under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC cannot provide advice because the request was made by a third party (the Director) rather than by Mr. Nicholson himself or his authorized representative, as required by Section 83114(b) and Regulation 18329.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declined to provide informal advice on conflict of interest duties because the request was submitted by a third party rather than by the interested party or his authorized representative. The FPPC referred the requester to relevant pamphlets and prior advice letters, and suggested filing a complaint if a violation was believed to have occurred.
